draft.introduction: Kaushitaki Upanishad 3 identifies consciousness (prajñā) as the essential nature of the Self. Without consciousness, speech cannot speak, the eye cannot see, the ear cannot hear.

translation.text: It is not speech that one should seek to know; one should know the speaker. It is not sight that one should seek to know; one should know the seer. It is not hearing that one should seek to know; one should know the hearer.
translation.original_text: na vācaṃ vijñāsīta vāktāraṃ vidyāt | na cakṣuṣ vijñāsīta draṣṭāraṃ vidyāt

translation.text: This one is Brahman, this one is Indra, this one is all the gods. These five great elements—earth, wind, space, water, light—the consciousness among all these is Brahman.
translation.original_text: eṣa brahmā eṣa indra eṣa sarve devāḥ

translator_notes.note: The shift from objects of knowledge to the knowing subject anticipates Kant by two millennia. The Upanishad insists that consciousness is not one faculty among many but the precondition for all faculties.
